title: Cat Constipation: What to Do if Your Cat Hasn't Pooped in 48 Hours
meta_desc: If your cat hasn't had a bowel movement in 48 hours, they may be constipated. Learn about symptoms, urgency levels, and when to see a vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat hasn't had a bowel movement in 48 hours, schedule a vet visit within 24 hours; however, immediate emergency care is required if they are also vomiting or straining unsuccessfully.

What is feline constipation and why is it painful for my cat?

Feline constipation occurs when stool moves too slowly through the digestive tract or becomes stuck in the colon. After 48 hours without a bowel movement, the waste typically becomes dry and hard, making it increasingly difficult and painful for your cat to pass naturally. This condition can lead to significant discomfort and potential long-term complications if left untreated.

How urgent is it if my cat hasn't had a bowel movement in 48 hours?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 hours if your cat has gone 48 hours without a bowel movement.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your cat is also vomiting, lethargic, or straining repeatedly without producing stool.
  • Watch for these signs as they may indicate a life-threatening intestinal or urinary blockage.

How does sharing a photo of the litter box help with my cat's triage?

  • Provide a photo of the litter box or any small, hard droppings to help the vet determine stool size and consistency.
  • Use visuals to help the medical team distinguish between straining to defecate and straining to urinate.
  • Identify critical emergencies, such as urinary blockages, which require immediate medical intervention.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For mild to moderate or recurrent constipation, enemas or manual extraction of impacted feces may be necessary. Suitable enemas include warm tap water or isotonic saline (5-10 ml/kg), with or without mild soap, dioctyl sodium sulfosuccinate (5-10 ml/cat), mineral oil (5-10 ml/cat), or lactulose (5-10 ml/cat), administered slowly. Phosphate-containing enemas must be avoided in cats. If enemas are unsuccessful, manual extraction under anesthesia with endotracheal intubation may be needed, potentially requiring multiple attempts over several days, alongside correction of fluid and electrolyte abnormalities. Dietary fiber supplementation with psyllium (1-4 tsp/meal), wheat bran (1-2 tbsp/meal), or pumpkin (1-4 tbsp/meal) added to canned food can also be beneficial, provided the animal is well-hydrated to prevent impaction. Pediatric rectal suppositories such as dioctyl sodium sulfosuccinate, glycerin, and bisacodyl can be used alone or with oral laxatives, requiring a compliant pet and willing owner.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 381)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat a human laxative?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your cat human medications or laxatives without specific veterinary guidance, as many of these products can be toxic or cause severe dehydration in felines.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of constipation in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include straining in the litter box, crying while attempting to go, producing very small and hard stools, or a sudden loss of appetite.

faqs.question: How does a vet treat constipation?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves fluid therapy to rehydrate the stool, stool softeners, or in more severe cases, a professional enema performed under veterinary supervision.
